question_title: Will Facebook or Instagram have a major outage (50,000+ peak Downdetector reports) between June 14 and June 26, 2026?
summary: The June 12, 2026 mega-outage (132K+ peak Downdetector reports) does NOT count as it started before the June 14 window. Services have been stable since June 14. The base rate for 50K+ peak Downdetector events is ~2-4/year, giving an unconditional 13-day probability of ~7-13%. A mild clustering uplift for mid-June infrastructure instability (unresolved root cause, users reported multiple outages that week) is partially offset by post-outage stabilization and the genuinely high 50K threshold that most outages never reach. Monte Carlo computation at rate=2.5/yr with 1.3x clustering multiplier yields ~10% for the remaining ~11 days. Final estimate: 11%.

factors.description: Base rate: 50K+ Downdetector peak events for Facebook/Instagram occur ~2-4 times per year historically. In a 13-day window, unconditional P ≈ 7-13%.
Clustering/infrastructure instability: Users reported 'third time this week' around June 12, suggesting mid-June infrastructure fragility. Root cause of June 12 outage not publicly disclosed by Meta.
Post-outage stabilization: Services have been operational for 2-3 days since June 12 mega-outage per IsItDownRightNow and StatusGator. Meta likely implemented emergency monitoring.
Very high threshold: 50,000+ peak is rare even among notable outages. Most 2026 outages (March 3: ~11K, April: ~14-18K) were well below threshold.
2026 data shows potentially lower rate: Only 1 event above 50K in ~5.5 months of 2026 data, suggesting annual rate may be closer to 2/yr than the historical 3-4/yr.
Remaining window still substantial: ~11 days remain in the resolution window, providing meaningful time for a new event to occur.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 11% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**
- The massive June 12 outage (132K+ peak reports) started *before* the window and doesn't count
- Facebook and Instagram have been stable since June 14 per monitoring services
- Historical base rate of 50K+ peak events is ~2-4/year → ~10% unconditional probability in a 13-day window
- Mild clustering uplift (multiple outages the week of June 9-12, undisclosed root cause) pushes slightly above base rate
- But the 50K threshold is genuinely high — most outages in 2026 peaked at only 11K-18K
- Post-outage stabilization efforts by Meta provide some downward pressure
- Net result: ~11%, slightly above the unconditional base rate but still firmly in "unlikely" territory
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Facebook or Instagram have a major outage (50,000+ peak Downdetector reports) between June 14 and June 26, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44061)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, for an outage incident beginning between 00:00 UTC on June 14, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on June 26, 2026, a credible source (see https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reports that Downdetector's peak number of user-submitted problem reports for either Facebook or Instagram reached at least 50,000 during that incident. The figure used must be a global (worldwide) Downdetector peak, not a single-country view. For example, a news report stating 'Facebook outage reports peaked at over 60,000 on Downdetector worldwide' resolves this question YES. Otherwise it resolves NO.
fine_print: 'Facebook' and 'Instagram' refer to Meta's social media platforms; outages of WhatsApp, Messenger, Threads, or Meta Ads/business tools do not count unless the same source also reports a global Facebook or Instagram peak of at least 50,000 Downdetector reports. The qualifying incident must begin within the stated window; an outage that began before 00:00 UTC June 14, 2026 does not count even if it continues into the window. Where a source reports a peak figure without specifying geography, it will be treated as a global figure unless the source indicates it is country-specific. If multiple credible sources cite differing global peak figures for the same incident, the highest such figure attributed to Downdetector governs. If no credible source reports a qualifying global Downdetector peak figure for Facebook or Instagram for an incident beginning within the window, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Base rate: 50K+ Downdetector peak events for Facebook/Instagram occur ~2-4 times per year historically. In a 13-day window, unconditional P ≈ 7-13%.
Clustering/infrastructure instability: Users reported 'third time this week' around June 12, suggesting mid-June infrastructure fragility. Root cause of June 12 outage not publicly disclosed.
Post-outage stabilization: Services have been operational for 2-3 days since June 12 mega-outage. Meta likely implemented emergency monitoring and fixes.
Very high threshold: 50,000+ peak is rare even among notable outages. Most 2026 outages (March 3: ~11K, April: ~14-18K) were well below threshold.
2026 data shows lower rate: Only 1 event above 50K in ~5.5 months of 2026 data, suggesting annual rate may be closer to 2/yr than 4/yr.
Remaining window still substantial: ~11 days remain in the resolution window, giving ample time for a new event to occur.

input.assessment:
The question asks about a 50K+ peak Downdetector event for Facebook or Instagram in a 13-day window (June 14-26, 2026). The June 12 mega-outage (132K+ peak) does NOT count as it started before the window. Services have been stable since June 14.

Base rate analysis: Historical data suggests 2-4 such events per year, giving an unconditional 13-day probability of ~7-13%. The 2026 data (only 1 qualifying event in 5.5 months) points toward the lower end. A mild clustering adjustment for mid-June infrastructure instability moves the estimate up slightly, but is partially offset by likely post-outage stabilization efforts by Meta.

The 50,000 threshold is genuinely high - most outages peak well below this even when they make the news. This pulls the estimate down from what a naive 'will there be any outage' probability would suggest.

My estimate of ~11% (logit -2.1) reflects a base rate around 8-10% with a slight upward push from clustering, moderated by the high threshold and post-outage monitoring.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is 10% based on midpoint historical rate of 3/yr. Final estimate of 11% is very close to anchor - the clustering uplift and stabilization downdraft roughly cancel, with a slight net upward push. 2026 data suggests rate may be lower (closer to 2/yr → 7%), which would pull estimate down. Hedging check: 11% is a reasonably confident 'unlikely but plausible' estimate, not artificially compressed toward 50%.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. True annual rate of 50K+ events - could be 1.5-4/yr depending on measurement. 2. Whether June clustering reflects an ongoing infrastructure issue or was resolved. 3. Whether Meta's post-outage response reduces or increases near-term risk.
input.tool_audit: Research tool successfully provided detailed data on the June 12 outage and current platform status. Historical base rate data was less precise (sub-agent couldn't access live web tools for frequency data). Web search confirmed current operational status. Downdetector live data not directly accessible but proxied through news and status monitoring sites.
input.update_triggers: 1. Reports of a new Facebook/Instagram outage in the June 14-26 window. 2. Reports of Meta infrastructure issues or engineering incidents. 3. Evidence that the June 12 root cause was not actually fixed.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for the June 12 event details but the historical frequency sub-agent couldn't access live tools, so the base rate estimate relies partly on training data. Direct web search helped confirm current status. The main friction was establishing precise base rates for 50K+ events - this specific threshold isn't commonly tracked in easily-findable datasets.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Resolution criteria**: Present and fully engaged. The agent fetched the Metaculus question via `get_metaculus_questions` and correctly identified that the June 12 outage (132K+ peak) does NOT count because it began before the June 14 window start. The factors and assessment explicitly address the 50,000 global Downdetector peak threshold, the credible-source requirement, and the window boundaries.

**Factor 1 (Base rate: 2-4 per year, logit=-1.8)**: Grounded in the research sub-agent's report, which synthesized historical data from training knowledge (Oct 2021, Mar 2019, Mar 2024, Jun 2019, Dec 2024). The sub-agent explicitly flagged that post-Jan-2025 data was unavailable via live tools — a noteworthy limitation but one the agent acknowledged. The question's own background_info corroborates that 50K+ events are rare. The 2-4/year estimate may be slightly generous relative to the named events (~5 in ~6 years), but the agent later adjusts downward using 2026-specific data (Factor 5), which compensates.

**Factor 2 (Clustering, logit=+0.5)**: Grounded. The Metaculus question background explicitly states users said it was the "third time Facebook has gone down this week" around June 12. CNET confirmed Meta had not disclosed the root cause. Correct positive direction.

**Factor 3 (Post-outage stabilization, logit=-0.3)**: Grounded. IsItDownRightNow data in the research trace confirms all-green status on June 14-15 with normal ping times (7-13ms). StatusGator web search results show only 53-61 user reports in past 24 hours. Correct negative direction.

**Factor 4 (Very high threshold, logit=-0.5)**: Grounded in the question background (March 3: ~11K, April: ~14-18K) and research data. Correct negative direction.

**Factor 5 (2026 lower rate, logit=-0.3)**: Grounded. Only 1 qualifying event (June 12) above 50K in ~5.5 months of 2026. Correct direction.

**Factor 6 (Remaining window, logit=+0.2)**: Straightforward arithmetic, correctly positive.

**No double-counting detected**: Factors 1 and 5 work together (historical rate adjusted by 2026-specific data). Factors 2 and 3 are opposing considerations on the same general topic (post-outage state) but draw on different evidence streams (user complaints about clustering vs. server status checks showing stability).

**No wrong-direction factors**: All logit signs match their descriptions.

**Contradictory assessment check**: Factor-implied logit is ~-1.53 (≈18% probability). Agent's final is -2.1 (11%). The ~7pp gap is acknowledged in the trace — the agent explicitly says it's weighting 2026-specific data more heavily. This is a deliberate judgment, not an accidental contradiction. The computational analysis supports 9.7% at rate=2.5/yr with 1.3x clustering, so 11% is squarely within the computed range.

**Anchor divergence**: Anchor is ~10%, final estimate is 11%. Minimal divergence, well-justified.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. The agent constructs a genuine alternative story: clustering may be underweighted (3 outages in 4 days ≠ Poisson), the 50K threshold may not be as rare as treated (two 50K+ events in recent 6 months), severity correlates with frequency during instability periods, and cites the October 2021 precedent of Meta going down again the same week.

**Confidence self-assessment**: 0.50 is appropriate for a question with uncertain base rates, limited live data access, and genuine uncertainty about whether infrastructure clustering elevates risk.

**Tool audit accuracy**: Agent states "8 calls, 0 errors" — consistent with ground truth metrics. The agent's caveat about the research sub-agent lacking live web access is confirmed by the research entry itself.

**Probability assessment**: 11% is consistent with the agent's own computation (9.7% at rate=2.5/yr, 1.3x multiplier). The estimate sits comfortably between the lower-bound (no clustering, lower rate: ~5-7%) and upper-bound (moderate clustering, higher rate: ~13-17%) ranges explored. I don't find reason to meaningfully disagree with this probability.

No errors found warranting a warn or fail.
